content.full_text:
CHRISTENSEN, BACIGALUPI & BARRUS
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
AN ASSOCIATION OF PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS

FIRST FLOOR
7112 NORTH FRESNO STREET
FRESNO, CALIFORNIA 93720
TELEPHONE (209) 431-6800
FAX (209) 431-4216

CRAIG G. CHRISTENSEN*
DALE E. BACIGALUPI*
JOHN E. BARRUS*
STEPHEN K. EHAT

8391 AUBURN BOULEVARD, SUITE 3
CITRUS HEIGHTS, CALIFORNIA 95610
TELEPHONE (916) 723-9000
FAX (916) 723-9012

*MEMBER OF A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

September 10, 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95812

Attention: Scott Hallabrin

Re: Request for Written Advice Pursuant to Government
Code Section 83114(b)

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

Your undersigned serves as city attorney for the City of
Parlier. The following set of facts presently exists and, under
the circumstances, I request your formal written advice on the
potential for violation of the Conflict of Interest laws.

The situation is as follows: Councilman John Trujillo owns a
tax and consulting business in Parlier. The business involves
certain bookkeeping and tax preparing functions for a variety of
clients. Several months ago one of the clients of Mr. Trujillo's
business complained to the Parlier Police Department that Mr.
Trujillo, in the course of performing certain contract work for the
complainant's business, had written certain checks without
authority and had embezzled funds of the business. The Parlier
Police Department, under the direction of the City police chief,
conducted an investigation of Mr. Trujillo and later turned the
matter over to the Fresno County District Attorney. The District
Attorney filed misdemeanor criminal charges against Mr. Trujillo
based on the police department investigation. Mr. Trujillo
maintained his innocence throughout the police department
investigation, and thereafter during the time the Fresno County
District Attorney handled the matter, and claimed that at all
times he had the full authority from the complaining business
owner to write all of the questioned checks to pay bills of the
complainant's company. Last month, the Fresno County District
Attorney dropped all charges for lack of evidence. After Mr.
Trujillo's defense counsel presented the exculpatory evidence to
the District Attorney and evidence of a less than thorough police
department investigation. In fact, there was apparently

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 10, 1992
Page 2

substantial evidence to corroborate Mr. Trujillo's explanation,
including evidence that the complaining party had been less than
truthful in the original complaint lodged with the police
department.

None of this exculpatory evidence had been turned up during
the police department investigation.

I have interviewed the assigned Deputy District Attorney as
well as Mr. Trujillo's defense counsel and learned that the police
chief had assigned a young and inexperienced officer to handle the
investigation; it also appears that during the time period just
preceding the dismissal of the charges, the detective assigned by
the police chief to investigate the matter notified several members
of the public that Mr. Trujillo had plead guilty to criminal
charges. This rumor was also repeated to several members of the
public by the police chief. In fact, these rumors were false and
Mr. Trujillo did not, at any time, publicly or privately, plead
guilty to any crime.

Councilman Trujillo has now notified the City that he believes
he may possess certain tort claims against the City (including but
not limited to those arising under the defamation laws) as a result
of the handling of the matter by the City police department, as
described above. Mr. Trujillo has also stated his belief that the
investigation was politically motivated.

The City of Parlier operates under a city administrator form
of government in which the police chief is hired by, evaluated by
and reports directly to the City Council; the City Council has
recently discussed the possibility of conducting an evaluation of
the police chief which might potentially lead to some form of
discipline involving the police chief as a result of the handling
of the matter. We are concerned about whether or not Councilman
Trujillo can legally participate in any executive session
discussions of the Parlier City Council, which may involve the
presence of the police chief, and in which discussions may be held
or decisions made involving (a) the police chief's proper or
improper negligent or non-negligent supervision of and personal
conduct during the criminal investigation of Mr. Trujillo and (b)
disciplinary action involving the police chief.

Would you please provide us written advice with respect to any
potential for violation of the Conflict of Interest laws by Mr.
Trujillo should he participate in any such meetings or decisions of
the Parlier City Council under the foregoing circumstances.

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 10, 1992
Page 3

Thank you for your cooperation.

Very truly yours,

DALE E. BACIGALUPI

DEB/hm

The situation is as follows: Councilman John Trujillo owns a tax and consulting business in Parlier. The business involves certain bookkeeping and tax preparing functions for a variety of clients. Several months ago one of the clients of Mr. Trujillo's business complained to the Parlier Police Department that Mr. Trujillo, in the course of performing certain contract work for the complainant's business, had written certain checks without authority and had embezzled funds of the business. The Parlier Police Department, under the direction of the City police chief, conducted an investigation of Mr. Trujillo and later turned the matter over to the Fresno County District Attorney. The District Attorney filed misdemeanor criminal charges against Mr. Trujillo based on the police department investigation. Mr. Trujillo maintained his innocence throughout the police department investigation, and thereafter during the time the Fresno County District Attorney handled the matter, and claimed that at all relevant times he had full authority from the complaining business owner to write all of the questioned checks to pay bills of the complainant's company. Last month, the Fresno County District Attorney dropped all charges for lack of evidence after Mr. Trujillo's defense counsel presented the exculpatory evidence to the District Attorney and evidence of a less than thorough police department investigation. In fact, there was apparently Fair Political Practices Commission September 10, 1992 Page 2 substantial evidence to corroborate Mr. Trujillo's explanation, including evidence that the complaining party had been less than truthful in the original complaint lodged with the police department. None of this exculpatory evidence had been turned up during the police department investigation. I have interviewed the assigned Deputy District Attorney as well as Mr. Trujillo's defense counsel and learned that the police chief had assigned a young and inexperienced officer to handle the investigation; it also appears that during the time period just preceding the dismissal of the charges, the detective assigned by the police chief to investigate the matter notified several members of the public that Mr. Trujillo had plead guilty to criminal charges.
